Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|burial mounds]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|burial mounds]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja

''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|burial mounds]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|burial mounds]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]]

Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya, Chiba]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 2]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|burial mounds]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|burial mounds]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} 玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|burial mounds]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|burial mounds]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|Kofuns]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|Kofuns]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|Kofuns]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|33.75|N|140|21|37.75|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Tamayori-hime no mikoto | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of [[Ichinomiya, Chiba|Ichinomiya]] in [[Chōsei District]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kazusa Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of [[Emperor Jimmu]] and daughter of the sea-dragon god [[Watatsumi]] ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|Kofuns]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]]. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the [[Satomi clan]] and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from [[Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sotobō Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Bronze mirror]]'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, [[Kamakura period]], designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|National Important Cultural Property]] in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of [[cupronickel]] (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}}
