However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the {{ill|Satomi clan|en|Satomi clan|WD=Q2975367|lt=Satomi clan}} and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from {{ill|Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|en|Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q564483|lt=Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sotobō Line|en|Sotobō Line|WD=Q670656|lt=Sotobō Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=National Important Cultural Property}} in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of {{ill|Cupronickel|en|Cupronickel|WD=Q649090|lt=Cupronickel}} (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Tamasaki-jinja}} [[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]] [[ja:玉前神社]] [[ko:다마사키 신사]] [[zh:玉前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q564302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Tamasaki-jinja]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]][[ja:玉前神社]][[ko:다마사키 신사]][[zh:玉前神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tamasaki-jinja]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]][[ja:玉前神社]][[ko:다마사키 신사]][[zh:玉前神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Tamasaki-jinja]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]][[ja:玉前神社]][[ko:다마사키 신사]][[zh:玉前神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = File:Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.37604167|N|140.36048611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Tamayori-bime|WD=Q3082751|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = September 13 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Ichinomiya|WD=Q1203602|1=Ichinomiya, Chiba}} }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Ichinomiya, Chiba|en|Ichinomiya, Chiba|WD=Q1203602|lt=Ichinomiya|1=Ichinomiya, Chiba}} in {{ill|Chōsei District|en|Chōsei District|WD=Q1203500|lt=Chōsei District|1=Chōsei District}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kazusa Province|en|Kazusa Province|WD=Q1055394|lt=Kazusa Province|1=Kazusa Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and daughter of the sea-dragon god {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the {{ill|Bōsō Peninsula|en|Bōsō Peninsula|WD=Q1022115|lt=Bōsō Peninsula|1=Bōsō Peninsula}} with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[:en:Shell midden|Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|Kofuns]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the {{ill|Satomi clan|en|Satomi clan|WD=Q2975367|lt=Satomi clan|1=Satomi clan}} and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from {{ill|Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|en|Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q564483|lt=Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|1=Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sotobō Line|en|Sotobō Line|WD=Q670656|lt=Sotobō Line|1=Sotobō Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=National Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of {{ill|Cupronickel|en|Cupronickel|WD=Q649090|lt=Cupronickel|1=Cupronickel}} (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Tamasaki-jinja}} [[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]] [[ja:玉前神社]] [[ko:다마사키 신사]] [[zh:玉前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q564302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Tamasaki-jinja]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]][[ja:玉前神社]][[ko:다마사키 신사]][[zh:玉前神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tamasaki-jinja]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]][[ja:玉前神社]][[ko:다마사키 신사]][[zh:玉前神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tamasaki-jinja]][[en:Tamasaki Shrine]][[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]][[ja:玉前神社]][[ko:다마사키 신사]][[zh:玉前神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamasaki Jinja
玉前神社 | image = File:Tamasaki-jinja.jpg | caption = Haiden of Tamasaki Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Chiba Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.37604167|N|140.36048611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Tamayori-bime|WD=Q3082751|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3048 Ichinomiya, Ichinomiya-machi, Chōsei-gun, Chiba-ken 299-4301 | website = {{Official|1=https://www.tamasaki.org/tamasaki_english/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = September 13 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Ichinomiya|WD=Q1203602|1=Ichinomiya, Chiba}} }} {{nihongo|'''Tamasaki Jinja'''|玉前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Ichinomiya, Chiba|en|Ichinomiya, Chiba|WD=Q1203602|lt=Ichinomiya|1=Ichinomiya, Chiba}} in {{ill|Chōsei District|en|Chōsei District|WD=Q1203500|lt=Chōsei District|1=Chōsei District}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kazusa Province|en|Kazusa Province|WD=Q1055394|lt=Kazusa Province|1=Kazusa Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 13, and features ''[[kagura]]'' performances, which are listed as an Intangible Cultural Property of Chiba PrefecturePlutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== *{{nihongo|'''[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]]'''|玉依姫命 |}}, the mother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and daughter of the sea-dragon god {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} ==History== The origins of Tamasaki Jinja are unknown. The shrine is located in an area of the {{ill|Bōsō Peninsula|en|Bōsō Peninsula|WD=Q1022115|lt=Bōsō Peninsula|1=Bōsō Peninsula}} with a favorable climate, which has been settled since at least the [[Jōmon period]]. [[:en:Shell midden|Shell midden]]s and [[kofun|Kofuns]] are common in the area. Its earliest appearance unhistorical documentation is an entry date 868 in the ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 877. The shrine is mentioned as the ''ichinomiya'' of Kazusa Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. However, repeated fires and other disasters over the centuries have destroyed all of the old shrine records and buildings. The shrine was burned down in 1562 during a battle involving the {{ill|Satomi clan|en|Satomi clan|WD=Q2975367|lt=Satomi clan|1=Satomi clan}} and was rebuilt by the Satomi in 1587. Additional structures were donated by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from {{ill|Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|en|Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q564483|lt=Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|1=Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sotobō Line|en|Sotobō Line|WD=Q670656|lt=Sotobō Line|1=Sotobō Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=National Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of {{ill|Cupronickel|en|Cupronickel|WD=Q649090|lt=Cupronickel|1=Cupronickel}} (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Tamasaki-jinja}} *{{Official|1=http://www.tamasaki.org/}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Tamasaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Tamasaki-jinja}} [[fr:Tamasaki-jinja]] [[ja:玉前神社]] [[ko:다마사키 신사]] [[zh:玉前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q564302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Additional structures were donated by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} in 1591 and the shrine reconstructed in 1687.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a seven-minute walk from {{ill|Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|en|Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q564483|lt=Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station|1=Kazusa-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Sotobō Line|en|Sotobō Line|WD=Q670656|lt=Sotobō Line|1=Sotobō Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Tamasaki-jinja, honden.JPG|Honden Tamasaki-jinja Kaguraden.jpg|Precincts 玉前神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den Tamasaki-jinja Gate.jpg|Ichi-no-torii ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}'''|梅樹双雀鏡|Baijusōjakukyō}}, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=National Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1953.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5574|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title= Baijusōjakukyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} The mirror has a diameter of 20.5 cm and a thickness of 3.5 cm, and is made of {{ill|Cupronickel|en|Cupronickel|WD=Q649090|lt=Cupronickel|1=Cupronickel}} (an alloy of copper and tin). The two small holes on the edge are presumed to indicate that this mirror was once hung in front of the shrine as a ''[[shintai]]''. On the reverse side is a design of running water with an old plum tree on the right and pampas grass to the left, with two sparrows plating on the trunk, which is bending left. This is an idealized spring scene typical of the Kamakura period.{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n141-005.html|title=梅樹双雀鏡|trans-title=Baijusōjakukyō|language=Japanese |publisher=Chiba Prefecture Department of Education |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society.
